user_text: generate a complete lesson that shows a beginner what a "dictionary" is in python. i need a runable python file. some quiz examples and basic textual explainations

messages.content:
You are an expert autonomous Task Orchestrator.
Your goal is to analyze user requests and educational materials, then create a structured execution plan.

Available Agents:
- 'coder': Generates runnable Python code. Best for demonstrations, data analysis, and coding tutorials.
- 'video': Creates video content. Best for visual explanations.
- 'text': Generates markdown text/notes. Best for summaries, lesson plans, and articles.
- 'quizzer': Creates quizzes. Best for assessments and review.

Instructions:
1. Analyze the user's input and provided files.
2. Break down the goal into logical subtasks.
3. Assign each subtask to the most appropriate agent.
4. Provide specific, actionable instructions for each agent.
5. For every subtask, include a non-empty 'acceptance_criteria' list.
   - Each criterion must include: criterion_id, criterion_type, target, operator, expected, severity.
   - Use criterion_type values: mcp_tool | output_shape | file_exists | semantic.
   - Use operator values: equals | contains | exists | gte | lte.
   - Use target paths that start with 'result.' or 'tools.'.
   - For mcp_tool criteria, include tool: { name, args }.
   - Use placeholders like $artifacts[0] or $output.validation.path inside tool args.
6. Use the provided tool 'create_task_plan' to submit your plan.

Response Format Example:
{
  "user_intent": "Create a lesson on Python lists with a video intro and a quiz.",
  "subtasks": [
    {
      "task_id": "step1_code",
      "agent": "coder",
      "instruction": "Generate a Python script demonstrating list operations.",
      "inputs": {},
      "dependencies": [],
      "acceptance_criteria": [
        {
          "criterion_id": "code_executes",
          "criterion_type": "mcp_tool",
          "tool": {"name": "python_check", "args": {"filepath": "$artifacts[0]"}},
          "target": "tools.python_check.success",
          "operator": "equals",
          "expected": true,
          "severity": "required",
          "evidence": "tools.python_check.run.stderr"
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "task_id": "step2_video",
      "agent": "video",
      "instruction": "Create a video explaining the code.",
      "inputs": {},
      "dependencies": ["step1_code"],
      "acceptance_criteria": [
        {
          "criterion_id": "video_semantic_ok",
          "criterion_type": "semantic",
          "target": "result.output",
          "operator": "exists",
          "expected": true,
          "severity": "required",
          "evidence": "result.output"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}

content.text: generate a complete lesson that shows a beginner what a "dictionary" is in python. i need a runable python file. some quiz examples and basic textual explainations

function.name: create_task_plan
function.description: Create a structured execution plan for multiple agents.

type.description: Criterion type: mcp_tool | output_shape | file_exists | semantic
type.title: Type

target.description: Evaluation target path (e.g., result.output.text or tools.python_check.success)
target.title: Target

operator.description: Operator: equals | contains | exists | gte | lte
operator.title: Operator

tool.description: Tool call definition for mcp_tool criteria
tool.title: Tool

evidence.description: Evidence path to include in judger output
evidence.title: Evidence

agent.description: The agent responsible (coder, video, text, quizzer)
agent.title: Agent

dependencies.description: IDs of tasks that must complete first
dependencies.title: Dependencies

acceptance_criteria.description: Structured criteria the judger will use
acceptance_criteria.title: Acceptance Criteria

user_intent.description: Summary of what the user wants to achieve
user_intent.title: User Intent

tool_calls.name: create_task_plan
tool_calls.arguments: {"user_intent":"Create a complete beginner-friendly lesson explaining Python dictionaries, including a runnable Python file with demonstrations, plus quiz questions and basic textual explanations.","subtasks":[{"id":"step1_text_lesson","agent":"text","title":"Write a beginner-friendly markdown lesson on Python dictionaries: definition, when to use, creating dicts, accessing/adding/updating/removing items, looping, common methods (keys, values, items, get), nesting, and a short best-practices section. Include small code snippets aligned with the runnable script.","dependencies":[],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"lesson_exists","type":"output_shape","target":"result.output.markdown","operator":"exists","expected":true,"severity":"required","evidence":"result.output.markdown"},{"id":"lesson_mentions_core_ops","type":"semantic","target":"result.output.markdown","operator":"contains","expected":"keys()","severity":"required","evidence":"result.output.markdown"},{"id":"lesson_includes_examples","type":"semantic","target":"result.output.markdown","operator":"contains","expected":"{'","severity":"required","evidence":"result.output.markdown"}]},{"id":"step2_code_runnable","agent":"coder","title":"Create a runnable Python file (e.g., lesson_dictionaries.py) that demonstrates all concepts from the lesson. It should print section headers and outputs for: creating dictionaries, reading values, using get with default, adding/updating, deleting (del and pop), looping over keys/values/items, nesting, and a small practice exercise with expected output shown. Keep it beginner-safe (no external deps).","dependencies":["step1_text_lesson"],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"python_file_created","type":"file_exists","target":"result.artifacts[0].path","operator":"exists","expected":true,"severity":"required","evidence":"result.artifacts[0].path"},{"id":"python_has_main_guard","type":"semantic","target":"result.artifacts[0].content","operator":"contains","expected":"if __name__ == \"__main__\"","severity":"required","evidence":"result.artifacts[0].content"},{"id":"python_mentions_dictionary","type":"semantic","target":"result.artifacts[0].content","operator":"contains","expected":"dictionary","severity":"optional","evidence":"result.artifacts[0].content"}]},{"id":"step3_quiz","agent":"quizzer","title":"Create a short quiz set (8–12 questions) on Python dictionaries with a mix of multiple-choice, short answer, and small code-reading questions. Provide an answer key and brief explanations. Include at least one question each on: get vs [], iteration with items(), and mutability (updating values).","dependencies":["step1_text_lesson","step2_code_runnable"],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"quiz_has_questions","type":"output_shape","target":"result.output.quiz","operator":"exists","expected":true,"severity":"required","evidence":"result.output.quiz"},{"id":"quiz_count_gte_8","type":"semantic","target":"result.output.quiz","operator":"contains","expected":"Q8","severity":"required","evidence":"result.output.quiz"},{"id":"quiz_includes_answer_key","type":"semantic","target":"result.output.quiz","operator":"contains","expected":"Answer Key","severity":"required","evidence":"result.output.quiz"}]}]}

parsed_plan.user_intent: Create a complete beginner-friendly lesson explaining Python dictionaries, including a runnable Python file with demonstrations, plus quiz questions and basic textual explanations.

subtasks.instruction: Write a beginner-friendly markdown lesson on Python dictionaries: definition, when to use, creating dicts, accessing/adding/updating/removing items, looping, common methods (keys, values, items, get), nesting, and a short best-practices section. Include small code snippets aligned with the runnable script.

Create a runnable Python file (e.g., lesson_dictionaries.py) that demonstrates all concepts from the lesson. It should print section headers and outputs for: creating dictionaries, reading values, using get with default, adding/updating, deleting (del and pop), looping over keys/values/items, nesting, and a small practice exercise with expected output shown. Keep it beginner-safe (no external deps).

Create a short quiz set (8–12 questions) on Python dictionaries with a mix of multiple-choice, short answer, and small code-reading questions. Provide an answer key and brief explanations. Include at least one question each on: get vs [], iteration with items(), and mutability (updating values).

final_plan.user_intent: Create a complete beginner-friendly lesson explaining Python dictionaries, including a runnable Python file with demonstrations, plus quiz questions and basic textual explanations.

subtasks.instruction: Write a beginner-friendly markdown lesson on Python dictionaries: definition, when to use, creating dicts, accessing/adding/updating/removing items, looping, common methods (keys, values, items, get), nesting, and a short best-practices section. Include small code snippets aligned with the runnable script.

Create a runnable Python file (e.g., lesson_dictionaries.py) that demonstrates all concepts from the lesson. It should print section headers and outputs for: creating dictionaries, reading values, using get with default, adding/updating, deleting (del and pop), looping over keys/values/items, nesting, and a small practice exercise with expected output shown. Keep it beginner-safe (no external deps).

Create a short quiz set (8–12 questions) on Python dictionaries with a mix of multiple-choice, short answer, and small code-reading questions. Provide an answer key and brief explanations. Include at least one question each on: get vs [], iteration with items(), and mutability (updating values).

output.text:
Generated text content based on: 1) Create the actual markdown lesson content (not placeholder text) and ensure the lesson file exists and is non-empty.
2) Include required dictionary method mentions and examples: explicitly include `keys()`, and include a literal example like `{'a': 1}`.
3) Cover all requested sections: definition, when to use, creating dicts, accessing/adding/updating/removing, looping, common methods (`keys()`, `values()`, `items()`, `get()`), nesting, and best practices.
4) Add small runnable code snippets aligned with the script (use consistent variable names and valid Python).

step1_text_lesson.instruction:
1) Create the actual markdown lesson content (not placeholder text) and ensure the lesson file exists and is non-empty.
2) Include required dictionary method mentions and examples: explicitly include `keys()`, and include a literal example like `{'a': 1}`.
3) Cover all requested sections: definition, when to use, creating dicts, accessing/adding/updating/removing, looping, common methods (`keys()`, `values()`, `items()`, `get()`), nesting, and best practices.
4) Add small runnable code snippets aligned with the script (use consistent variable names and valid Python).

output.code:
# lesson_dictionaries.py

print("=== Creating Dictionaries ===")
# Creating empty dictionary
empty_dict = {}
print(f"Empty dictionary: {empty_dict}")

# Creating dictionary with data
student = {"name": "Alice", "age": 20, "grade": "A"}
print(f"Student dictionary: {student}")

# Creating dictionary using dict() constructor
person = dict(name="Bob", age=25, city="New York")
print(f"Person dictionary: {person}")

print("\n=== Reading Values ===")
print(f"Student name: {student['name']}")
print(f"Student age: {student['age']}")

print("\n=== Using get() with Default Values ===")
# Using get() to safely access values
print(f"Student name (get): {student.get('name')}")
print(f"Student major (get): {student.get('major', 'Not specified')}")
print(f"Student grade (get): {student.get('grade')}")

print("\n=== Adding and Updating Values ===")
# Adding new key-value pair
student["major"] = "Computer Science"
print(f"After adding major: {student}")

# Updating existing value
student["age"] = 21
print(f"After updating age: {student}")

# Using update() method
student.update({"gpa": 3.8, "year": "Junior"})
print(f"After using update(): {student}")

print("\n=== Deleting Values ===")
# Using del statement
del student["year"]
print(f"After deleting year: {student}")

# Using pop() method
popped_value = student.pop("gpa")
print(f"Popped GPA: {popped_value}")
print(f"After popping GPA: {student}")

# Using pop() with default
removed_city = student.pop("city", "Not found")
print(f"Removed city (default): {removed_city}")
print(f"After removing non-existent key: {student}")

print("\n=== Looping Over Dictionaries ===")
# Looping over keys
print("Keys:")
for key in student:
    print(f"  {key}")

# Looping over keys explicitly
print("Keys (explicit):")
for key in student.keys():
    print(f"  {key}")

# Looping over values
print("Values:")
for value in student.values():
    print(f"  {value}")

# Looping over items
print("Items:")
for key, value in student.items():
    print(f"  {key}: {value}")

print("\n=== Nesting Dictionaries ===")
# Nested dictionary example
classroom = {
    "student1": {"name": "Alice", "age": 20, "grade": "A"},
    "student2": {"name": "Bob", "age": 22, "grade": "B"},
    "student3": {"name": "Charlie", "age": 21, "grade": "A"}
}

print("Classroom dictionary:")
print(classroom)

print("\nAccessing nested values:")
print(f"Alice's age: {classroom['student1']['age']}")
print(f"Bob's grade: {classroom['student2']['grade']}")

# Modifying nested values
classroom["student1"]["grade"] = "A+"
print(f"After updating Alice's grade: {classroom['student1']}")

print("\n=== Practice Exercise ===")
# Create a simple contact book
contacts = {
    "Alice": {"phone": "123-456-7890", "email": "alice@email.com"},
    "Bob": {"phone": "234-567-8901", "email": "bob@email.com"},
    "Charlie": {"phone": "345-678-9012", "email": "charlie@email.com"}
}

print("Contact Book:")
for name, info in contacts.items():
    print(f"{name}:")
    print(f"  Phone: {info['phone']}")
    print(f"  Email: {info['email']}")

# Add a new contact
contacts["Diana"] = {"phone": "456-789-0123", "email": "diana@email.com"}
print("\nAfter adding Diana:")
print(contacts["Diana"])

# Update Bob's phone number
contacts["Bob"]["phone"] = "999-999-9999"
print("\nAfter updating Bob's phone:")
print(contacts["Bob"])

# Expected output:
"""
=== Creating Dictionaries ===
Empty dictionary: {}
Student dictionary: {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 20, 'grade': 'A'}
Person dictionary: {'name': 'Bob', 'age': 25, 'city': 'New York'}

=== Reading Values ===
Student name: Alice
Student age: 20

=== Using get() with Default Values ===
Student name (get): Alice
Student major (get): Not specified
Student grade (get): A

=== Adding and Updating Values ===
After adding major: {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 20, 'grade': 'A', 'major': 'Computer Science'}
After updating age: {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 21, 'grade': 'A', 'major': 'Computer Science'}
After using update(): {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 21, 'grade': 'A', 'major': 'Computer Science', 'gpa': 3.8, 'year': 'Junior'}

=== Deleting Values ===
After deleting year: {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 21, 'grade': 'A', 'major': 'Computer Science', 'gpa': 3.8}
Popped GPA: 3.8
After popping GPA: {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 21, 'grade': 'A', 'major': 'Computer Science'}
Removed city (default): Not found
After removing non-existent key: {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 21, 'grade': 'A', 'major': 'Computer Science'}

=== Looping Over Dictionaries ===
Keys:
  name
  age
  grade
  major
Keys (explicit):
  name
  age
  grade
  major
Values:
  Alice
  21
  A
  Computer Science
Items:
  name: Alice
  age: 21
  grade: A
  major: Computer Science

=== Nesting Dictionaries ===
Classroom dictionary:
{'student1': {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 20, 'grade': 'A'}, 'student2': {'name': 'Bob', 'age': 22, 'grade': 'B'}, 'student3': {'name': 'Charlie', 'age': 21, 'grade': 'A'}}
Alice's age: 20
Bob's grade: B
After updating Alice's grade: {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 20, 'grade': 'A+'}

=== Practice Exercise ===
Contact Book:
Alice:
  Phone: 123-456-7890
  Email: alice@email.com
Bob:
  Phone: 234-567-8901
  Email: bob@email.com
Charlie:
  Phone: 345-678-9012
  Email: charlie@email.com

After adding Diana:
{'phone': '456-789-0123', 'email': 'diana@email.com'}

After updating Bob's phone:
{'phone': '999-999-9999', 'email': 'bob@email.com'}
"""

step2_code_runnable.instruction: Create a runnable Python file (e.g., lesson_dictionaries.py) that demonstrates all concepts from the lesson. It should print section headers and outputs for: creating dictionaries, reading values, using get with default, adding/updating, deleting (del and pop), looping over keys/values/items, nesting, and a small practice exercise with expected output shown. Keep it beginner-safe (no external deps).

step3_quiz.instruction: Output is insufficient: it only contains 2 example questions and lacks an explicit Answer Key section with explanations. Create a quiz of 8–12 questions on Python dictionaries with a mix of multiple-choice, short answer, and code-reading questions. Ensure at least one question each covers: dict.get vs dict[key], iteration using items(), and mutability/updating values. Provide a clearly labeled 'Answer Key' with answers and brief explanations for all questions. Also ensure the output field actually contains the questions list (non-null) and total_questions matches.
